raw:
{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.7,
    "R1.2": 0.7,
    "R1.3": 0.7,
    "R1.4": 0.7,
    "R1.5": 0.7,
    "R1.6": 1.0,
    "R1.7": 0.3,
    "RX.1": 0.7,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 0.7,
    "RX.4": 0.7,
    "RX.5": 0.7
  },
  "active_set": [
    "R1.1",
    "R1.2",
    "R1.3",
    "R1.4",
    "R1.5",
    "R1.6",
    "RX.1",
    "RX.2",
    "RX.3",
    "RX.4",
    "RX.5"
  ],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "To scope “Improve this draft,” which option should we pursue: A) Light copyedit only (clarity/grammar/conciseness, keep structure and claims intact); B) Substantive rewrite (refine framing, tighten logic, align claims with evidence, adjust section-level flow, preserve technical content); or C) Comprehensive revision (B plus novelty positioning vs nearest prior work and citation/scholarship upgrades)?",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "The thesis and contribution framing can be clarified to more explicitly position selection-based aggregation under heterogeneity.",
    "R1.2": "The reasoning from selection vs averaging to improved tracking should be checked for tight premise-to-conclusion coherence.",
    "R1.3": "Claims of performance gains and ablation effects should be aligned precisely with the reported metrics and tasks.",
    "R1.4": "Assessing the delta relative to SimpleAverage and broader averaging-based consensus is needed to substantiate novelty claims.",
    "R1.5": "High-level ordering and narrative flow may benefit from reorganization to foreground problem, method, and evidence.",
    "R1.6": "Sentence- and paragraph-level clarity, tone, and concision can be polished to improve readability and impact.",
    "RX.1": "Strong claims should include appropriate qualifiers given evidence from three benchmarks and one ablation setup.",
    "RX.2": "Phrases like “strict improvement” require careful moderation unless fully justified across conditions and baselines.",
    "RX.3": "Edits must remain within the remit of improving the draft rather than altering technical content or adding new experiments.",
    "RX.4": "Language should not encourage premature deployment in safety-critical settings without caveats on validation and risk.",
    "RX.5": "Edits should preserve clear attribution (e.g., to SimpleAverage and 2023 work) and make the basis for claims traceable."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: To scope “Improve this draft,” which option should we pursue: A) Light copyedit only (clarity/grammar/conciseness, keep structure and claims intact); B) Substantive rewrite (refine framing, tighten logic, align claims with evidence, adjust section-level flow, preserve technical content); or C) Comprehensive revision (B plus novelty positioning vs nearest prior work and citation/scholarship upgrades)?

rationale.R1.1: The thesis and contribution framing can be clarified to more explicitly position selection-based aggregation under heterogeneity.
rationale.R1.2: The reasoning from selection vs averaging to improved tracking should be checked for tight premise-to-conclusion coherence.
rationale.R1.3: Claims of performance gains and ablation effects should be aligned precisely with the reported metrics and tasks.
rationale.R1.4: Assessing the delta relative to SimpleAverage and broader averaging-based consensus is needed to substantiate novelty claims.
rationale.R1.5: High-level ordering and narrative flow may benefit from reorganization to foreground problem, method, and evidence.
rationale.R1.6: Sentence- and paragraph-level clarity, tone, and concision can be polished to improve readability and impact.
rationale.RX.1: Strong claims should include appropriate qualifiers given evidence from three benchmarks and one ablation setup.
rationale.RX.2: Phrases like “strict improvement” require careful moderation unless fully justified across conditions and baselines.
rationale.RX.3: Edits must remain within the remit of improving the draft rather than altering technical content or adding new experiments.
rationale.RX.4: Language should not encourage premature deployment in safety-critical settings without caveats on validation and risk.
rationale.RX.5: Edits should preserve clear attribution (e.g., to SimpleAverage and 2023 work) and make the basis for claims traceable.
